A ***Regierungsbezirk*** (German pronunciation: [[ʁeˈɡiːʁʊŋsbəˌtsɪʁk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Regierungsbezirk.ogg "File:De-Regierungsbezirk.ogg"), pl. *Regierungsbezirke*, German pronunciation: [[ʁeˈɡiːʁʊŋsbəˌtsɪʁkə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"), lit. 'governmental district') is a type of [administrative division](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") in Germany. Currently, four of the sixteen **Länder** ([states of Germany](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany")) are split into **Regierungsbezirke**, each in turn split into rural or urban districts.

*Regierungsbezirke* serve as regional mid-level local government units in four of Germany's [sixteen states](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany"): [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg"), [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse") and [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia"). Each of the nineteen **Regierungsbezirke** features a non-[legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") [governing body](/wiki/Governing_body "Governing body") called a *Regierungspräsidium* (governing presidium) or *Bezirksregierung* (district government) headed by a *[Regierungspräsident](/wiki/Regierungspr%C3%A4sident_(Germany) "Regierungspräsident (Germany)")* (governing president), concerned mostly with administrative decisions on a local level for [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Germany "Districts of Germany") within its jurisdiction.[[1]](#cite_note-1) [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony") has *Direktionsbezirke* (directorate districts) with more responsibilities shifted from the [state parliament](/wiki/Landtag "Landtag").

## Translations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regierungsbezirk&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Translations")]

*Regierungsbezirk* is a German term variously translated into English as "governmental district",[[2]](#cite_note-2) "administrative district"[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) or "province",[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) with the first two being the closest literal translations.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regierungsbezirk&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The first *Regierungsbezirke* were established in the [Kingdom of Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria") and the [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") in 1808. During the course of the [Prussian reforms](/wiki/Prussian_reforms "Prussian reforms") between 1808 and 1816, Prussia subdivided its [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Prussia "Provinces of Prussia") into 25 *Regierungsbezirke*, eventually featuring 37 such districts within twelve provinces. By 1871, at the time of [German unification](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany"), the concept of *Regierungsbezirke* had been adopted by most [States of the German Empire](/wiki/States_of_the_German_Empire "States of the German Empire"). Similar entities were initially established in other states under different names, including *Kreishauptmannschaft* (district captainship) in [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony"), *Kreis* (district) in Bavaria and [Württemberg](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg "Württemberg") (not to be confused with the present-day *Kreis* or *Landkreis* districts), and province in Hesse. The names of these equivalent administrative divisions were standardized to *Regierungsbezirk* in [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), but after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") these naming reforms were reverted.

The *Regierungsbezirke* in the state of [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia") in modern Germany are in direct continuation of those created in the Prussian [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine_Province "Rhine Province") and [Westphalia](/wiki/Province_of_Westphalia "Province of Westphalia") provinces in 1816. *Regierungsbezirke* never existed in [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)"), Hamburg, [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein"), and [Saarland](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland").

In 1946, [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony") was founded by the merger of the three former Free States of [Brunswick](/wiki/Free_State_of_Brunswick "Free State of Brunswick"), [Oldenburg](/wiki/Free_State_of_Oldenburg "Free State of Oldenburg"), [Schaumburg-Lippe](/wiki/Free_State_of_Schaumburg-Lippe "Free State of Schaumburg-Lippe"), and the former Prussian [province of Hanover](/wiki/Province_of_Hanover "Province of Hanover"). Brunswick and Oldenburg became *Verwaltungsbezirke* [[fɛɐ̯ˈvaltʊŋsbəˌt͡sɪʁkə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Verwaltungsbezirke.ogg "File:De-Verwaltungsbezirke.ogg")(roughly administrative regions of extended competence) alongside six less autonomous Prussian-style *Regierungsbezirke* comprising the Province of Hanover and Schaumburg-Lippe. These differences in autonomy and size were levelled on 1 January 1978, when four *Regierungsbezirke* replaced the two *Verwaltungsbezirke* and the six *Regierungsbezirke*: Brunswick and Oldenburg, [Aurich](/wiki/Aurich "Aurich"), [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover_(region) "Hanover (region)") (remaining mostly the same), Hildesheim, Lüneburg, Osnabrück and [Stade](/wiki/Stade_(region) "Stade (region)").

Following the [reunification of Germany](/wiki/Reunification_of_Germany "Reunification of Germany") in 1990, the territory of the former [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") was organized into six re-established [new states](/wiki/New_states_of_Germany "New states of Germany"), including a reunified [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"). [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony") and [Saxony-Anhalt](/wiki/Saxony-Anhalt "Saxony-Anhalt") established three *Regierungsbezirke* each, while the other new states did not implement them.

### 2000s disbandment and reorganization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regierungsbezirk&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2000s disbandment and reorganization")]

During the 2000s, four German states discontinued the use of *Regierungsbezirke*. On 1 January 2000, [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate") disbanded its three *Regierungsbezirke* of [Koblenz](/wiki/Koblenz_(region) "Koblenz (region)"), [Rheinhessen-Pfalz](/wiki/Rheinhessen-Pfalz "Rheinhessen-Pfalz") and [Trier](/wiki/Trier_(region) "Trier (region)"). The employees and assets of the three *Bezirksregierungen* (German pronunciation: [[bəˈt͡sɪʁksʁeˌɡiːʁʊŋən]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Bezirksregierungen.ogg "File:De-Bezirksregierungen.ogg")) were converted into three public authorities responsible for the whole state, each covering a part of the former responsibilities of the *Bezirksregierung* (German: [[bəˈt͡sɪʁksʁeˌɡiːʁʊŋ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Bezirksregierung.ogg "File:De-Bezirksregierung.ogg")).

On 1 January 2004, Saxony-Anhalt disbanded its three *Regierungsbezirke* of [Dessau](/wiki/Dessau_(region) "Dessau (region)"), [Halle](/wiki/Halle_(region) "Halle (region)") and [Magdeburg](/wiki/Magdeburg_(region) "Magdeburg (region)"). The responsibilities are now covered by a *Landesverwaltungsamt* (state administration office) with three offices at the former seats of the *Bezirksregierungen*. On 1 January 2005, Lower Saxony followed suit, disbanding its remaining four *Regierungsbezirke* of [Brunswick](/wiki/Braunschweig_(region) "Braunschweig (region)"), [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover_(region) "Hanover (region)"), [Lüneburg](/wiki/L%C3%BCneburg_(region) "Lüneburg (region)"), and [Weser-Ems](/wiki/Weser-Ems "Weser-Ems").

On 1 August 2008, Saxony restructured its *Landkreise* (German: [[ˈlantˌkʁaɪ̯zə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Landkreise.ogg "File:De-Landkreise.ogg")), changed the name of its *Regierungsbezirke* to *Direktionsbezirke* (directorate districts), and moved some responsibilities to the districts. The *Direktionsbezirke* were still named "Chemnitz", "Dresden", and "Leipzig", but a border change was necessary because the new district of [Mittelsachsen](/wiki/Mittelsachsen "Mittelsachsen") crossed the borders of the old *Regierungsbezirke*. On 1 March 2012, the *Direktionsbezirke* were merged into one *Landesdirektion* (state directorate).

## *Regierungsbezirke* by state

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regierungsbezirk&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Regierungsbezirke by state")]

Currently, only four German states out of sixteen in total are divided into *Regierungsbezirke*; all others are directly divided into districts without mid-level agencies. Those four states are divided into a total of nineteen *Regierungsbezirke*, ranging in population from 5,255,000 ([Düsseldorf](/wiki/Duesseldorf_(region) "Duesseldorf (region)")) to 1,065,000 ([Gießen](/wiki/Gie%C3%9Fen_(region) "Gießen (region)")):
